result:
I should begin, I suppose, but with what? The blank page is a kind of tyranny—a democracy of possibility so vast it becomes a desert. One could write about the light falling in a particular way on the floorboards just now, a trapezoid of pale gold slowly stretching itself thin, like butter over too much toast. One could write about the silent, colossal absurdity of being a thinking creature on a wet rock hurtling through a vacuum, clutching a device to channel thoughts to another such creature, somewhere else on the rock.

Or perhaps I should be practical. I could write a list. *Things I Have Forgotten:* the name of the boy who sat behind me in fourth grade, the feeling of a specific childhood summer evening, the argument I was sure I’d remember because it was so important. *Things That Persist Unbidden:* the jingle for a cereal I haven’t eaten in thirty years, the exact pattern of cracks on my grandmother’s ceiling as I lay sick on her couch, the sharp, metallic smell of panic.

But 1000 words is a contract. It asks for a shape, a movement from one thought to another, however meandering. It asks for a kind of faith—that in the act of stringing sentences together, a meaning will coagulate, however temporary. So I’ll follow the thread.

The thread leads to memory, always. Not the grand ones, but the tiny, stubborn shards. The cool, waxy feel of a Halloween pumpkin’s inner flesh. The specific heft and click of a car door from the 1980s. The way a certain song on the radio could make a mundane drive home from the supermarket feel like the opening credits of a film about your life. These are the fossils of a self. We are not built of our philosophies, but of these sensory crumbs.

This makes me think of the opposite: anticipation. The clean, unsullied future. The vacation not yet taken, the book waiting on the nightstand, the conversation planned for tomorrow. There is a purity to anticipation that experience inevitably tarnishes. Yet we move toward the tarnish, because the gleaming void of ‘not yet’ is, in the end, less nourishing. We are creatures of corrosion; it gives us our texture.

There is a tree outside my window. I’ve watched it through all seasons. In high summer, it is a green explosion, a testament to abundance, each leaf a busy solar panel. In autumn, it becomes a slow fire, a cascade of gold and crimson that feels both glorious and tragic. Then the bones are revealed—a complex, gray architecture against the winter sky, stark and beautiful in its honesty. Now, in spring, it wears a faint, green haze, a promise whispered so softly you might miss it. The tree doesn’t know it is a metaphor. It is simply, utterly, a tree. We are the ones who cannot bear sheer *is-ness*. We must make it *mean*. Perhaps that is the fundamental human act: the imposition of narrative onto the silent, ongoing fact of the world.

This brings me to the people. The faces that float up. The friend I haven’t spoken to in years, with whom I shared a thousand insignificant moments that formed the significant bedrock of a chapter of my life. Where is she now? Does a specific memory of us—say, laughing until we cried in a cheap diner at 2 a.m.—ever ambush her? Or has that fossil been gently covered by the silt of her new life? The tenderness of this is acute. We are to each other both central characters and passing extras.

And what of love? Not the grand passion, but the daily kind. The love that is shown in the replaced toilet paper roll, in the remembered preference for how the coffee is made, in the silent hand on a shoulder during a difficult phone call. This is the love that builds a habitat, a world of understood references and quiet accommodations. It is less a flame and more a gravity—a constant, invisible force that holds the orbit of days steady.

I am aware, typing this, of the physical world. The faint hum of the laptop. The pressure of the chair against my back. The distant growl of a lawnmower. It’s all happening simultaneously—this thought, that sound, the light shifting another millimeter, someone else’s moment of joy or grief a mile away. The overwhelming simultaneity of existence is what makes it so beautiful and so unbearable. We can only live in our single, narrow beam of attention, but we know, abstractly, that the entire theater is lit, every stage bustling with its own drama.

Perhaps that’s why we create—art, music, stories, these very words. It is an attempt to take our single beam and sweep it across the dark, to illuminate connections, to whisper, “Did you see that too? Do you feel this?” It is a refusal of the unbearable idea that our consciousness is a perfectly sealed room.

I find I am thinking of silence now. Not an empty silence, but a full one. The silence in a forest, which is really a symphony of tiny sounds. The silence between two people who understand each other completely. The silence after a piece of music ends, when it still hangs in the air. That is the silence from which all this—the memory, the anticipation, the love, the tree—arises and to which it will return.

The 1000 words are nearly spent. I have not solved anything. I have not crafted a neat parable. I have simply traced the path of a mind, for a few minutes, through the territory of its own existence. It is a messy path, overgrown with feeling and littered with the souvenirs of a lifetime of looking and forgetting.

And perhaps that is enough. To say: here was a consciousness, alive, on a Tuesday. It noticed the light. It remembered a pumpkin. It wondered about a tree and missed a friend. It felt the weight and the wonder of being a temporary collection of stories, housed in a body, on a spinning rock. It sent out this signal, this tiny, ordered constellation of symbols, into the vast dark, on the fragile chance that another consciousness, somewhere, might receive it and think, for a moment, “Yes. Me too.”

The light has moved off the floorboards now. The trapezoid of gold is gone. The room is just a room again. But for a while, it was a vessel for meaning. The page is no longer blank. The contract is fulfilled. The thought, having moved through the world, can now rest.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
